```text
$ docker exec openclaw-62682-proof-8f484bcd cat /tmp/gateway2.log | grep -E "loading|starting|ready"
2026-05-22T04:33:03.168+00:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-22T04:33:03.384+00:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-22T04:33:06.882+00:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
2026-05-22T04:33:10.199+00:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 6.8s)
2026-05-22T04:33:11.571+00:00 [gateway] ready
```

In-container proof driver (runs INSIDE the openclaw-62682-proof-8f484bcd container, imports `runWithModelFallback` from the deployed `/workspace/dist/model-fallback-*.js`):

```text
$ docker exec openclaw-62682-proof-8f484bcd node /tmp/proof.mjs

[bundle] using model-fallback-C3IGEhKZ.js
[bundle] contains 'OPENCLAW_ABORTABLE_WRAPPER'                          : true
[bundle] contains 'isOpenClawAbortableWrapper'                          : true
[bundle] contains 'cron: isolated agent run stalled before execution start' : true
[bundle] contains 'cron: isolated agent setup timed out before runner start' : true
[bundle] contains 'cron: job execution timed out'                       : true

=== A5. AbortError(cause: TimeoutError) WITH abortable() marker — terminal ===
  run() calls   = 1 (expected 1)
  outcome       = AbortError: run budget exhausted
  PASS

[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=primary-provider/primary-model reason=rate_limit next=ollama/fb1-model detail=provider rate limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb1-model reason=rate_limit next=ollama/fb2-model detail=provider rate limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb2-model reason=rate_limit next=ollama/fb3-model detail=provider rate limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb3-model reason=rate_limit next=none detail=provider rate limited

=== B1. RateLimitError (non-terminal provider failure) — cascade 4 times ===
  run() calls   = 4 (expected 4)
  outcome       = FallbackSummaryError: All models failed (4): primary-provider/primary-model: provider rate limited (rate_limit) | ...
  PASS

[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=primary-provider/primary-model reason=timeout next=ollama/fb1-model detail=aborted
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb1-model reason=timeout next=ollama/fb2-model detail=aborted
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb2-model reason=timeout next=ollama/fb3-model detail=aborted
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-provider/primary-model candidate=ollama/fb3-model reason=timeout next=none detail=aborted

=== B2. PROVIDER-STYLE AbortError(cause: TimeoutError) WITHOUT marker — cascade 4 times ===
  run() calls   = 4 (expected 4)
  outcome       = FallbackSummaryError: All models failed (4): primary-provider/primary-model: aborted (timeout) | ollama/fb1-model: aborted (timeout) | ...
  PASS

Summary:
  PASS  A1-A4: cron prefix variants → 1 call each (regression sanity)
  PASS  A5: AbortError(cause: TimeoutError) WITH marker (run-budget shape) → 1 call (terminal)
  PASS  B1: RateLimitError → 4 calls cascade (provider-retry semantics)
  PASS  B2: AbortError(cause: TimeoutError) WITHOUT marker (provider-style) → 4 calls cascade (BOT'S P1 CONCERN ADDRESSED)
```

The A5/B2 contrast is the key new evidence for the marker fix: both shapes are byte-identical except for the `OPENCLAW_ABORTABLE_WRAPPER` symbol marker. A5 (marker present) → terminal short-circuit. B2 (marker absent, simulating a provider SDK wrapper) → emits the exact 4 `[model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed reason=timeout next=...` log lines, cascading through every configured candidate just like before this PR. Per-provider retryable timeout semantics are preserved by design, not by accident.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - All 70 tests in `model-fallback.test.ts` pass after the change
  - All 20 tests in `model-fallback.probe.test.ts` pass
  - All 8 tests in `agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts` pass
  - End-to-end smoke: container rebuilt from this branch, HTTP client disconnect on `/v1/responses` produces the expected `[openresponses] client disconnected, aborting streaming run runId=...` log line and the agent run terminates within ~1s (the upstream `watchClientDisconnect` plumbing already in `aad3bbedd` works correctly with the new `ClientDisconnectError` reason tag)
- Edge cases checked: cause-chain walking (one level deep) for `TimeoutError`/`ClientDisconnectError` wrapped inside an outer `AbortError` -- this matches `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:1387`'s `makeAbortError` pattern
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Direct E2E of the fallback-skip path with a real cron timeout firing (would require setting `timeoutSeconds` to a very small value; the unit tests cover the contract)
  - The image-model fallback variant (`runWithImageModelFallback`) -- it gets the new `abortSignal?` parameter for consistency but no callers currently pass a signal

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** -- the new `abortSignal?` parameter is optional. Existing callers that don't pass it continue to behave exactly as before.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A future caller passes an abort signal whose `signal.reason` happens to have `name === "TimeoutError"` for an unrelated reason, accidentally short-circuiting the fallback chain when they didn't want to.
  - **Mitigation**: The check is intentionally narrow -- only the exact name strings `TimeoutError` and `ClientDisconnectError` match. Both names are already reserved for run-budget timeout (set by `makeTimeoutAbortReason` in `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`) and HTTP client disconnect (set by `watchClientDisconnect` in `gateway/http-common.ts` after this PR).
- **Risk**: `TimeoutError` from a per-provider request timeout (not run-budget) gets mistaken for a run-budget timeout and skips fallback when it shouldn't.
  - **Mitigation**: Per-provider timeouts come from the LLM SDK's internal fetch timeout, which throws an error directly -- they don't propagate through `runAbortController.abort()`. Only `runAbortController` is tagged with `TimeoutError` via `makeTimeoutAbortReason`.

## Out of scope (for separate PRs)

- Issue #37505 (cron timeout aborts entire fallback chain via shared AbortController) -- different abort source (cron service `executeJobCoreWithTimeout`), needs per-attempt AbortController isolation. PR #42482 already addresses this.
- Issue #58578 (fallback chain aborted by premature primary restore mid-flight) -- different abort source (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`), needs coordination state between live-model-switch and fallback layers

## Relationship to PR #52365

PR #52365 (fix(cron): stop fallback attempts when cron budget is exhausted) addresses the same underlying problem from #60388 but with a different, complementary mechanism:

- **#52365 is proactive**: a new `beforeAttempt` hook in `runWithModelFallback` that lets the cron layer check its remaining budget *before* each attempt and stop the chain if budget is too low.
- **This PR is reactive**: an `isTerminalAbort(signal)` check in `shouldRethrowAbort` that inspects `signal.reason` *after* an attempt aborts to decide whether to rethrow or retry.

These are complementary, not exclusive. #52365's `beforeAttempt` hook stops the chain *before* wasting an attempt when budget is known to be low. This PR's check stops the chain *after* the first attempt aborts with a terminal reason -- which covers both the cron-timeout case (redundantly with #52365) AND the HTTP client disconnect case (which #52365 does not address).

Notable differences:

- **Scope**: this PR is 8 files / ~276 lines touching only `model-fallback.ts`, `http-common.ts`, `agent-command.ts`, 3 auto-reply callers, and one cron caller. #52365 is 47 files / ~1900 lines (includes unrelated slack/plugin-sdk changes).
- **Client disconnect coverage**: this PR handles the HTTP `/v1/responses` and `/v1/chat/completions` client-disconnect case by tagging the abort with `ClientDisconnectError` in `watchClientDisconnect`. #52365 does not touch the gateway HTTP path.
- **Detection layer**: this PR checks `signal.reason` which is a generic mechanism usable by *any* caller (including future non-cron sources). #52365's `beforeAttempt` hook requires each caller to implement its own budget-check logic.

Either PR alone fixes the #60388 cron-timeout case. Both merged together would give defense-in-depth: `beforeAttempt` stops before wasting an attempt when budget is known to be low, and `isTerminalAbort` stops after any attempt aborts with a terminal reason (including client disconnects that the cron-aware hook doesn't see).

If the maintainers prefer #52365's approach and would rather not have two overlapping mechanisms, I'd suggest retargeting this PR to cover only the `ClientDisconnectError` branch of `isTerminalAbort` (the unique coverage) and letting #52365 handle the cron-timeout case via its proactive hook.

target.title: fix(sandbox): bump config hash when buildSandboxCreateArgs flags change
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH` constant and a `createArgsEpoch` field to both `SandboxHashInput` and `SandboxBrowserHashInput`, so the existing config-hash recreate path also fires when `buildSandboxCreateArgs` adds, removes, or changes container creation flags.

**This PR adds the mechanism only.** It does not ship any flag change itself. Whichever PR adds the first real flag change (for example #74083 adding `--init`) bumps the epoch from the bootstrap value in the same release as the flag.

## Why

`buildSandboxCreateArgs` already drives container recreation via the labelled `openclaw.configHash`, but the hash inputs only capture mount layout (`mountFormatVersion`) and browser security flags (`securityEpoch`). Other docker create flags are invisible to the hash. So adding any new flag in a future PR (`--init`, cgroup limits, sysctls, capabilities, namespace flags, etc.) does NOT invalidate the hash, and existing containers keep running with their old flags forever.

The most-affected pattern is exactly the one #68691 reports: long-running sandbox containers (`scope: "agent"` + `prune.maxAgeDays: 0`) stay alive for weeks and never recreate naturally. Without an epoch in the hash, they never pick up new flags.

## What this PR adds

1. **`SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH`** in `src/agents/sandbox/constants.ts`, set to a neutral bootstrap value (`"2026-05-bootstrap"`) and date-tagged so reviewers can tell at a glance which release introduced the bump.
2. **Header comment on the constant** explicitly telling future contributors to bump the epoch in the same PR (or a follow-up PR) as the actual flag change, never speculatively. Pre-bumping under a name like `"init-tini"` would let containers be recreated with a hash claiming the new behaviour while the actual create args still lack it.
3. **`createArgsEpoch: string`** in both `SandboxHashInput` and `SandboxBrowserHashInput`. Mirrors the existing `securityEpoch` / `mountFormatVersion` pattern.
4. **Both call sites updated** (`docker.ts:ensureSandboxContainer`, `browser.ts:ensureSandboxBrowser`) to pass the new constant.
5. **`openclaw.createArgsEpoch=...` container label** stamped at create time, alongside the existing `openclaw.mountFormatVersion` label, for diagnostics.
6. **Tests** (`config-hash.test.ts`, `docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts`) updated; new test "changes when docker create args epoch changes" mirrors the existing security-epoch test using neutral `"epoch-v1"`/`"epoch-v2"` sentinels.
7. **CHANGELOG** entry under `## Unreleased > ### Fixes` describing the mechanism, with `Thanks @aaajiao.` per repo convention.

## Reusability

When future PRs add or change container flags, the contributor only needs to bump the `SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH` string in the same change and add a one-line history entry to the comment. No new types, no new test scaffolding, no risk of forgetting to invalidate the hash.

## Relationship to #74083 (and #68691)

Originally I had named this epoch after the `--init` rollout. Per clawsweeper P1 review, that was wrong — it would let this PR claim a feature ship that lives in a separate PR. The current value is neutral; the `--init` rollout in #74083 is responsible for bumping the epoch when it lands. With both merged in the same release, the long-running-container pattern reported in #68691 is fully addressed.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/config-hash.test.ts` — existing + new tests pass locally
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts` — recreate path still asserts hash mismatch triggers `docker rm -f`
- [ ] Maintainer-side: full CI pass (note: the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-cli` failures in `plugins-location-bridges.test.ts` reproduce on upstream/main — see commit `5ecd01ff9` — and have no overlap with sandbox)
- [ ] Live verification: a future flag change (such as #74083) bumps the epoch and a long-running `scope: "agent"` sandbox is recreated on next agent run

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(diagnostics): rotate cache trace and payload JSONL logs
target.body:
## Summary
- Add opt-in archive rotation to the queued JSONL writer while preserving cap-and-drop behavior for existing maxFileBytes-only callers.
- Bound diagnostic JSONL sinks by default: cache trace rotates at 50 MB with 3 archives; Anthropic provider payload logs rotate at 100 MB with 5 archives.
- Add config/env wiring, generated schema metadata, docs, and changelog entries for the new caps and archive retention controls.

## Impact
This is an operational safety fix. In real local usage, cache-trace JSONL reached 46 GB after only a couple months of OpenClaw use. Because these diagnostic files had no rotation or cap, temporary forensic tracing could silently become a disk and memory pressure source, and large trace files can strain tools during restart/recovery enough to freeze or destabilize a setup.

## Testing
- pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/queued-file-writer.test.ts src/agents/cache-trace.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-payload-log.test.ts src/config/logging-max-file-bytes.test.ts src/infra/dotenv.test.ts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts
- pnpm config:schema:check
- pnpm config:docs:check
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed TS files>
- env OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_PREPARE=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.core.json <changed TS files>
- pnpm tsgo:core:test

Fixes #75380.
Related: #41676, #74801, #73655, #57031.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011
Fixes #82304

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Guard every run_doctor caller**: the existing-config fresh-install path called `run_doctor` without checking its return value, so a failed doctor would still set `should_open_dashboard=true`. Now guarded with `if run_doctor; then should_open_dashboard=true; fi`
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** (1) Pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer; the installer continues and opens a dead dashboard. (2) `guardCancel` in onboard-helpers exited with 0/1 instead of 130, so the installer could not distinguish user cancellation from regular failure.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v26.3.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-76386`. Gateway started and doctor command exercised on a real OpenClaw deployment.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from branch: `CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`

  Step 2. Verified the fix in the compiled production bundle (before vs after).

  Step 3. Started the patched gateway and ran `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` to confirm the doctor module works correctly.

  Step 4. Sent SIGINT to the installer during a real upgrade to verify the abort path.

  Step 5. Ran the full install script test suite (49 tests).

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  **Before (upstream/main):** `guardCancel` exits with 0, making the installer treat Clack cancellation as success:

  ```console
  $ sed -n '27,31p' /tmp/oc-base/dist/onboard-helpers-DxyrQbNM.js
  function guardCancel(value, runtime) {
  if (isCancel(value)) {
  cancel(stylePromptTitle("Setup cancelled.") ?? "Setup cancelled.");
  runtime.exit(0);
  throw new Error("unreachable");
  ```

  **After (patched):** `guardCancel` exits with 130 (SIGINT convention), which the installer detects and aborts:

  ```console
  $ sed -n '27,31p' /tmp/wt-76386/dist/onboard-helpers-D47vq5rt.js
  function guardCancel(value, runtime) {
  if (isCancel(value)) {
  cancel(stylePromptTitle("Setup cancelled.") ?? "Setup cancelled.");
  runtime.exit(130);
  throw new Error("unreachable");
  ```

  **Install script `run_doctor` now detects exit 130:**

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'doctor_exit.*130\|abort_install_int' /tmp/wt-76386/scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2835:    if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  2836:        abort_install_int
  3303:            if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  ```

  **Doctor runs correctly on the live deployment:**

  ```console
  $ cd /tmp/wt-76386 && timeout 5 node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive
  OPENCLAW
  ```

target.title: fix(exec): hide policy controls from model args
target.body:
## Summary

- Remove operator-only exec controls (`security`, `ask`, `host`, `elevated`, `node`) from the model-facing exec tool schema
- Stop consuming those fields from exec tool-call args; configured tool defaults and approval defaults now remain the only policy source
- Move trusted chat command policy overrides into exec defaults instead of per-call args
- Update regression coverage for model-supplied policy fields, configured host/node routing, elevated defaults, and agent-specific exec defaults

Fixes #75811.

## Security

This closes the model-controlled exec policy boundary described in #75811. Model tool calls can still provide command/runtime inputs (`command`, `workdir`, `env`, `yieldMs`, `background`, `timeout`, `pty`), but cannot lower/raise security, approval, host routing, elevation, or node selection through tool arguments.

## Test plan

- pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts

target.title: fix(slack): make tool-progress draft preview ephemeral; preserve agent text segments
target.body:
## Problem

The Slack `Working…\n• …` draft preview is meant to scaffold tool-execution visibility while an agent runs. Today, those scaffolding messages linger as separate Slack messages every time the agent emits a new assistant-message segment mid-turn, or finalizes after a tool burst. A 5-step turn ends up with ~5 `Working…` preview messages plus the final answer — visible fan-out across boundaries.

Telegram's lane stream already handles this: it edits one message and finalizes to the answer. This PR brings Slack to parity by making the draft preview fully ephemeral while preserving every agent text segment as a permanent Slack message.

## Approach

Tracks a per-draft kind: `'narrative' | 'bullets' | null`. Transitions:

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| narrative → bullets | rotate (`forceNewMessage`); preserve narrative as a permanent Slack message |
| bullets → narrative | `await draftStream.clear()` (chat.delete); start a fresh draft for narrative |
| boundary, narrative | rotate (preserve) |
| boundary, bullets | clear (delete) |
| `kind=final` + bullets | clear + `deliverNormally` (answer is a fresh permanent message) |
| cleanup, bullets still open | clear |

The bullets→narrative transition AWAITs `clear()` (and so makes `updateDraftFromPartial` async) to avoid a race where the throttled draft loop could `chat.update` the bullet message with narrative text BEFORE `clear`'s synchronous step zeroed the cached `messageId` — and then `clear`'s `chat.delete` would proceed against the (now-narrative) message, destroying the narrative.

`shouldEnableSlackPreviewStreaming` also drops the carve-out that kept DM-without-thread off. Without that, the preview never appears in the most common Slack scenario (DMs with the bot, where replies don't auto-thread by default), and every mid-turn tool/block payload falls back to a fresh `chat.postMessage` instead.

## End state for a typical turn
- Each agent text segment posts as a permanent Slack message that edits in real time as tokens stream in (unchanged from before).
- Tool calls render as a single `Working…\n• …` bullet preview that edits via `chat.update` as new tools fire (unchanged from before).
- When a tool burst ends and the agent emits more text — or the turn finalizes — the bullet preview is `chat.delete`'d. The channel ends the turn with only the agent's text content.

## Tests
- Updated `dispatch.streaming.test.ts` to reflect the always-on preview-streaming behaviour for non-off modes (3 cases adjusted, no new cases needed).
- **89 files / 894 tests pass**.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` clean.

## Compatibility
- No new dependencies, no API surface changes, no config changes.
- The `streaming.preview.toolProgress` config flag still gates whether bullets are rendered at all — when off, the new ephemeral logic is dormant (no bullets to delete).
- Apps registered as Slack 'Agents & AI Apps' using `chat.startStream` are unaffected (`useStreaming` short-circuits before the draft path).

This was developed against `blink-new/openclaw` (a small downstream fork) and validated end-to-end on production Blink Claw agents on Fly.io across multiple iterations.

target.title: fix: harden subagent completion announce cleanup races
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a set of subagent completion lifecycle races that can leave completed child runs stuck behind cleanup/announce sequencing or overwrite a terminal error with a later success signal.

The patch makes three related changes:

- Do not block completion announce delivery on browser-session cleanup. The cleanup is now started after the announce/cleanup flow is leased and runs best-effort in the background, with warnings captured instead of blocking the lifecycle path.
- Skip waiting for an active embedded child session when a frozen final reply is already available. If we already captured the reply text, the requester can be notified immediately instead of waiting on a stale/active child run check.
- Preserve a terminal error outcome if a later completion signal reports success for the same run, unless the run was actually restarted after the error. This prevents cleanup/finalization races from turning a failed run into a successful task after the fact.

## Why this matters

The lifecycle path has a few asynchronous pieces that can finish in different orders:

1. child session/browser cleanup
2. completion announce delivery back to the requester
3. task finalization bookkeeping
4. embedded child-run settle checks
5. terminal lifecycle hooks

Before this patch, a slow or stuck browser cleanup could delay the announce/cleanup flow. Separately, if a child run had already produced frozen reply text but still appeared active, the announce flow could wait unnecessarily. And in a failure path, a later `complete` event could overwrite the already-recorded terminal error.

That combination makes subagent completion feel flaky from the user's side: the work is done, but the parent session may not receive the completion promptly, or the persisted final task state can become misleading.

## Implementation notes

### Lifecycle cleanup lease

`completeSubagentRun` now preclaims cleanup when `triggerCleanup` is true and cleanup has not already completed/been handled. That lease is passed into `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` as `skipBeginCleanup`, avoiding a second `beginSubagentCleanup` race while still protecting duplicate cleanup attempts.

If cleanup is not actually triggered, or announce is suppressed for steer-restart, the preclaim is released so the record does not stay permanently handled.

### Browser cleanup no longer blocks announce cleanup

Previously browser cleanup was awaited before starting the subagent announce cleanup flow. This patch starts the announce cleanup flow first, retires run-mode bundle MCP runtime as before, and then launches browser cleanup as a best-effort async task:

- uses injected `cleanupBrowserSessionsForLifecycleEnd` when provided
- lazy-loads the browser cleanup helper otherwise
- logs warning context on failure
- does not reject or delay completion delivery

### Frozen reply short-circuit

`runSubagentAnnounceFlow` now checks whether `roundOneReply` already contains non-empty frozen reply text. If it does, the flow does not wait for `waitForEmbeddedPiRunEnd` even if `isEmbeddedPiRunActive` reports true. The frozen reply is already the durable completion payload; waiting only increases the chance of a stale active-state race.

### Terminal error preservation

When a run already has a terminal error outcome/reason, a later successful completion signal no longer overwrites it unless the entry's `startedAt` proves the run restarted after the error's `endedAt`. The ended hook and persisted outcome use the preserved effective reason/outcome.

## Tests

Added/updated coverage for:

- announcing without waiting for browser cleanup
- skipping announce delivery when completion messages are disabled
- delete-mode cleanup when completion messages are disabled
- preserving terminal errors against later success completion
- allowing success to replace error only after a restart
- using frozen reply text without waiting on an active embedded child session

Verification run locally:

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts
```

Result: 2 files passed, 29 tests passed.

target.title: feat(cli): add openclaw experimental for toggling experimental config flags
target.body:
> AI-assisted. Tested on pushed branch `115daf62fe`: focused Vitest coverage for config/CLI/chat/TUI passed, config schema check passed, the real `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` entrypoint resolved, the interactive CLI picker wrote a throwaway config with visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif), a real TUI selector flow has visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif), and a rebuilt Docker gateway exercised `/experimental` over WebSocket with persisted config writes.

## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw has experimental config flags spread through the config schema, but the user-facing toggle surface was drifting toward a CLI-only implementation. That would make `/experimental` unavailable from chat/TUI/channel command surfaces and would duplicate the flag registry outside the config contract.
- **Why it matters:** Experimental flags affect runtime behavior and should use one bounded, auditable config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel plugins instead of hard-coded per-surface lists.
- **What changed:** Added a shared experimental-flag helper that discovers boolean config leaves under `experimental` from the base config schema and schema UI hints. The CLI `openclaw experimental`, chat `/experimental`, TUI command handling, and channel-command registry now use that same subset.
- **Config command policy:** Added `commands.experimental` so `/experimental` can be enabled or disabled alongside other configurable command surfaces.
- **What did not change:** No new experimental flags were introduced. No secrets, network calls, or provider runtime behavior changed.

## Scope Note

This PR started as a small CLI picker, but review feedback exposed a broader product/security boundary: if experimental config can be edited from one surface, the implementation should not create a CLI-only registry or drift from chat, TUI, and channel command handling. The current shape is intentionally larger because it moves `/experimental` behind a shared schema-derived subset and a configurable command gate instead of adding another one-off config editor.

The scope is still kept to the minimum shared surface needed for that boundary: no new experimental flags, no arbitrary config editor, no new network/runtime capability, no SDK export, and no separate per-surface experimental registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CLI
- [x] Config
- [x] Messaging / channel command surface
- [x] TUI
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Discord suggestion from Json in #general
- Reviewer concern addressed: Peter Steinberger asked that this avoid config-schema drift, consider `/experimental` in chat/TUI, and keep config editing security-bounded.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A - new feature and follow-up design correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

Focused local tests cover the shared flag registry, CLI apply behavior, chat command parsing/gating, channel command listing, and TUI command handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New top-level command: `openclaw experimental`
- New slash command: `/experimental`
- TUI command handling can list and update experimental flags through the same shared registry
- `commands.experimental` controls whether the experimental chat/channel command is enabled
- Experimental flags are discovered from the config schema instead of duplicated in each surface

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Config schema + UI hints
        |
        v
shared experimental flag subset
        |
        +-- openclaw experimental
        +-- /experimental in chat/channel command handling
        +-- TUI command backend/handlers
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No. The command writes only existing config paths that users can already edit through config tooling.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, `/experimental` is a new configurable command surface. It is gated by `commands.experimental` and is limited to schema-discovered boolean config leaves whose path contains an `experimental` segment.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- Security boundary: this does not make arbitrary config editable from chat/TUI. The editable subset is derived from the config schema and narrowed to experimental boolean leaves only.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu source checkout in Codex worktree
- Runtime: Node 22 / pnpm
- Branch SHA: `115daf62fe`
- Docker proof image: `openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest` (image `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`)
- Docker proof gateway: `pr76298-exp-proof-gateway`, `127.0.0.1:19982->18789`, health `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`

### Commands run locally

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=180000 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/config/experimental-flags.test.ts src/config/experimental-config-file.test.ts src/commands/experimental.test.ts src/auto-reply/commands-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-gating.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-parse.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts
git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm openclaw experimental --help
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental
jq . /tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json
OPENCLAW_IMAGE=openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest docker compose -p pr76298-exp-proof build openclaw-gateway
docker run -d --name pr76298-exp-proof-gateway -p 127.0.0.1:19982:18789 ... openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest node dist/index.js gateway --bind lan --port 18789
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19982/healthz
docker exec pr76298-exp-proof-gateway node --input-type=module -e '<WebSocket /experimental proof script>'
jq . /tmp/openclaw-pr76298-docker-proof/config/openclaw.json
```

### Expected

The schema-derived experimental flag subset is stable, CLI/chat/TUI surfaces use it consistently, `/experimental` obeys command gating, the real CLI entrypoint resolves the new command, and a gateway client with the right operator scopes can list and toggle the bounded experimental config subset.

### Actual

Verified on pushed branch `115daf62fe`.

## Evidence

- [x] Focused Vitest run passed 5 shards / 128 tests after the final type-only commit.
- [x] `git diff --check` passed before the final commit; `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed for the final type-only commit.
- [x] `pnpm config:schema:check` passed (`[base-config-schema] ok`).
- [x] `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` executed through the real repo CLI entrypoint at SHA `c354c59`.
- [x] Interactive CLI picker executed on head `115daf6`, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, printed the updated-config summary, and persisted the expected throwaway config. Slow VHS GIF: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif
- [x] TUI selector proof executed through the real `runTui` loop with an isolated fixture backend. The TUI opened `/experimental`, listed experimental flags, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, printed `experimental enabled: agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and logged `setExperimentalFlag`. VHS GIF: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif
- [x] Docker image rebuilt after the final commit and the proof container ran from image `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`.
- [x] Dockered gateway health returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`.
- [x] WebSocket `chat.send` proof through the gateway returned real `Config updated` replies for `/experimental off tools.experimental.planTool` and `/experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`.
- [x] Persisted config contains `tools.experimental.planTool: false`, `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: true`, and `meta.lastTouchedVersion: "2026.5.22"`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: schema-derived flag listing/apply helpers, CLI command behavior, chat parse/gating/list/set behavior, TUI command handling, embedded trusted-local TUI config path, live CLI registration/help output, interactive CLI picker write behavior, real TUI selector behavior with an isolated backend, and Dockered gateway command behavior over WebSocket.
- Edge cases checked: disabled command gating, unknown flag selectors, no-op flag updates, invalid snapshots, validation failures, absent unchecked picker flags becoming explicit `false`, and boolean-only experimental subset discovery.
- What was not verified: native Telegram visible proof. The Dockered gateway proof exercises the shared chat/native command handler through the gateway protocol, but it is not a screenshot or transcript from a real Telegram channel.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: `openclaw experimental` and `/experimental` now use one schema-derived, bounded experimental config subset across CLI, chat/gateway, TUI, and channel-command handling. The latest patch also fixes mixed-case completion matching and narrows the quick-toggle docs wording.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu source checkout at `115daf62fe`; Docker gateway behavior proof captured at `c354c598f7` before the final type-only TUI backend interface commit; rebuilt Docker gateway image `openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest` with image id `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`; container `pr76298-exp-proof-gateway` bound to `127.0.0.1:19982->18789` with isolated config under `/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-docker-proof/config/openclaw.json`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran focused Vitest through `node scripts/test-projects.mjs`, `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`, prod/test typecheck wrappers, lint wrapper, extension lint/package-boundary checks, `pnpm config:schema:check`, `pnpm openclaw experimental --help`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental`, rebuilt the Docker gateway with `OPENCLAW_IMAGE=openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest docker compose -p pr76298-exp-proof build openclaw-gateway`, started a clean proof container, checked `/healthz`, then connected over WebSocket as `gateway-client` with `operator.write` and `operator.admin` scopes and sent `/experimental off tools.experimental.planTool`, `/experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and `/experimental list`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
Focused Vitest after final commit: passed 5 shards / 128 tests
Prod typecheck after final commit: core and extensions passed
Test typecheck after final commit: core and extensions passed
Lint after final commit: core/extensions/scripts passed
Extension boundary after final commit: channel lint, bundled lint, package compile, and package canary passed
Schema check: [base-config-schema] ok
CLI help: OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (c354c59), Usage: openclaw experimental [options]
Gateway health: {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
CONNECT {"ok":true,"scopes":["operator.write","operator.admin"]}
FINAL /experimental off tools.experimental.planTool: Config updated: tools.experimental.planTool=false
FINAL /experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: Config updated: agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean=true
Interactive CLI picker slow VHS proof: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif
TUI selector VHS proof: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif
TUI backend log: listExperimentalFlags returned the three schema-derived flags; setExperimentalFlag wrote agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean=true changed=true.
Interactive CLI picker on head 115daf6:
Updated config:
  enabled agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean
  disabled agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory
  disabled tools.experimental.planTool
Persisted picker config: localModelLean=true, sessionMemory=false, planTool=false, lastTouchedVersion=2026.5.22
FINAL /experimental list:
- on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean - Enable Lean Local Model Mode (Experimental)
- off agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory - Memory Search Session Index (Experimental)
- off tools.experimental.planTool - Enable Structured Plan Tool
```

Persisted config excerpt after the gateway proof:

```json
{
  "commands": { "experimental": true },
  "tools": { "experimental": { "planTool": false } },
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.22",
    "lastTouchedAt": "2026-05-24T03:40:31.959Z"
  },
  "agents": {
    "defaults": { "experimental": { "localModelLean": true } }
  }
}
```

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add replyToOfflineMessages config to process missed messages after reconnect
target.body:
## Problem

Messages received during WhatsApp disconnects are **silently lost**. When the gateway reconnects, offline messages arrive with `upsert.type === "append"` (history sync) and are **skipped** by default.

This causes:
- DM messages lost after reconnect
- Group messages stop working after gateway restart
- Blue ticks sent but no reply (sender sees message read but ignored)

**15+ issues** trace back to the same `append` skip.

## Solution

Add opt-in config to process offline messages:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    whatsapp: {
      replyToOfflineMessages: true,
      offlineMessageMaxAgeSeconds: 300, // default: 300 (5 minutes)
    },
  },
}
```

- `replyToOfflineMessages: true` — Process offline messages instead of skipping
- `offlineMessageMaxAgeSeconds: 300` — Skip messages older than 5 minutes (prevent flooding)

## Related Issues

- **#50093** — WhatsApp: Backfill missed messages after reconnection
- **#66904** — Support WhatsApp history sync on reconnect (closed as duplicate)
- **#19856** — Messages lost after 408 reconnect
- **#1619** — Messages lost during disconnect
- **#18672** — Messages dropped after prekey renegotiation
- **#1952, #2767, #20952** — Group messages stop after restart
- **#14069** — Group messages stop after relinking

## Based On

This PR is based on **#21719** by @faahim which was closed by stale bot due to inactivity.

The original PR had:
- ✅ Complete solution
- ✅ Tests passing
- ✅ Docs updated
- ✅ 15+ issues identified

## Changes

1. `src/config/types.whatsapp.ts` — Add config fields
2. `extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.ts` — Add resolved fields
3. `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts` — Implement logic
4. `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts` — Pass options

## Default Behavior

**Unchanged** — By default, offline messages are still skipped (`replyToOfflineMessages: false`). Users must opt-in to process them.

---

**Context:** OpenClaw WhatsApp bundled user since 2026.3.13, currently on latest version.

target.title: fix: add --timeout flag to openclaw message send with SIGTERM support
target.body:
## Summary

Add a `--timeout <ms>` flag to `openclaw message send` (default: 30000ms) so that delivery requests can no longer hang indefinitely. Also adds SIGTERM/SIGINT handling in `executeGatewayRequestWithScopes` with proper listener cleanup so external supervisors like `GNU timeout(1)` can cleanly interrupt stuck requests.

## Problem

When `openclaw message send` calls the gateway and the channel delivery hangs (e.g. Telegram rate limit, network issue), the CLI process:
- Has no internal timeout — waits forever
- Ignores SIGTERM from external `timeout` command
- Consumes 60-70% CPU indefinitely

**Impact:** Cron jobs calling `openclaw message send` become unsafe; one stuck delivery can cascade into gateway/agent pileups.

## Fix

1. **register.send.ts** — Add `--timeout <ms>` option (default: 30000)
2. **helpers.ts** — Parse timeout, validate (reject NaN/negative/zero), pass as `timeoutMs`
3. **message.ts** — Inject `timeoutMs` into the gateway params passed to `runMessageAction`
4. **call.ts** — Add SIGTERM/SIGINT handler in `executeGatewayRequestWithScopes` using `process.on` (not `process.once`) with proper `process.off` cleanup in the `stop` function so listeners don't leak after each gateway call
5. **call.test.ts** — Add test verifying signal listeners are disposed after gateway request settles
6. **CHANGELOG.md**, **docs/cli/message.md** — Document the new flag

## Testing

### Unit tests

```
pnpm test src/gateway/call.test.ts src/cli/program/message/helpers.test.ts src/commands/message.test.ts

✓ gateway (79 tests) — includes "disposes SIGTERM/SIGINT listeners after gateway request settles"
✓ cli (23 tests)
✓ commands (5 tests)
```

### Signal listener cleanup (standalone proof)

Without cleanup, listeners persist after normal path:
```
onHelloOk -> stop() -> resolve() -> SIGTERM/SIGINT remain registered = leak
```

Proof that cleanup works:
```
$ node -e "
const h = () => {};
const bS = process.listenerCount('SIGTERM');  // 0
const bI = process.listenerCount('SIGINT');   // 0
process.on('SIGTERM', h); process.on('SIGINT', h);
process.off('SIGTERM', h); process.off('SIGINT', h);
console.log('SIGTERM:', process.listenerCount('SIGTERM')); // 0
console.log('SIGINT:', process.listenerCount('SIGINT'));   // 0
"
SIGTERM: 0
SIGINT: 0
```

### Timeout input validation

| Input | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `--timeout 5000` | timeoutMs: 5000 |
| `--timeout -100` | timeoutMs: undefined (falls to default 30000) |
| `--timeout 0` | timeoutMs: undefined (falls to default 30000) |
| `--timeout abc` | timeoutMs: undefined (falls to default 30000) |

Fixes #75895

target.title: Feat/discord slash command deploy 75888
target.body:
## Summary
Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:
If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.
- **Problem:** Discord native slash-command deploy lacked a configurable lifecycle policy (`always` / `changed-only` / `disabled`), durable fingerprints across gateway restarts for `changed-only`, safe in-process coordination when multiple Discord accounts trigger background slash deploy from one gateway. Separately: Vitest 4 removed **`TestRunner`** from **`vitest`**, breaking the repo custom non-isolated runner; Discord provider harness mocks needed the new **`resolveDiscordSlashCommandDeployConfig`** export.
- **Why it matters:** Fewer redundant Discord application-command REST calls when unchanged (rate-limit / churn), explicit opt-out (`disabled`), restart-stable `changed-only` semantics, and predictable multi-account deploy ordering; tests must boot on current Vitest.
- **What changed (product/code):** `channels.discord.slashCommandDeploy` accepts string shorthand **or** `{ mode }` (default **`changed-only`** when omitted); merge persist successful deploy fingerprints; preload deployer snapshot for `changed-only`; queue per-process background deploy; docs (`docs/channels/discord.md`), changelog, config UI hints, core types + zod schema + plugin-sdk runtime helper alignment, regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`, new/updated Discord extension tests around deploy state and wiring.
- **What changed (test infra):** `test/non-isolated-runner.ts` extends **`VitestTestRunner`** from **`vitest/runners`**; `extensions/discord/src/test-support/provider.test-support.ts` mocks **`resolveDiscordSlashCommandDeployConfig`** for `monitor/commands.js` partial mock compatibility.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Non-Discord channels/providers; unrelated gateway loops beyond shared config/schema surfaces required for the new key.
_AI-assisted._ Local verification: targeted Discord extension Vitest shard (see **Repro + Verification → Actual**).
## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Default when omitted: **`changed-only`**. **`changed-only`** persists successful deploy fingerprints across restarts to skip redundant REST deploy when command definitions are unchanged. Multi-account Discord background slash deploy is serialized per gateway process. Docs/changelog describe behavior; config baseline hash regenerated for schema/docs drift checks.

## Risks and Mitigations
- **Risk:** Fleet-wide cold start may still issue full deploys until first successful fingerprint write exists per application/account pairing.
  - **Mitigation:** `changed-only` amortizes after first success; `disabled` / `always` explicit operator posture.

## Real behavior proof

  - Behavior addressed:
    Discord `slashCommandDeploy=changed-only` persists deploy fingerprints in Discord plugin state (SQLite) and skips redundant Discord application-command REST on gateway restart when the serialized command set is unchanged.

  - Real environment tested:
    macOS (darwin arm64), Node v24.14.0; **live Discord gateway** on branch `feat/discord-slash-command-deploy-75888` @ **`7bf4b50`** (`OpenClaw 2026.6.1` local build), guild `1471499665584296039`, application `1471498116237885474`, `--profile discord-proof`, token via `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` env (not pasted in PR).

  - Exact steps or command run after this patch:
    1. `pnpm build`
    2. `source ~/.env/third-party-apis.zsh && export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=~/.openclaw-discord-proof`
    3. **Prep (cold fingerprint):** `sqlite3 ~/.openclaw-discord-proof/state/openclaw.sqlite "DELETE FROM plugin_state_entries WHERE plugin_id='discord' AND namespace='slash-command-deploy-hashes';"`
    4. **RUN1:** `node openclaw.mjs --log-level debug --profile discord-proof gateway run --verbose --port 19099 --force` — wait for `deploy-commands:done`
    5. Stop gateway; verify plugin-state fingerprint row exists (see Evidence)
    6. **RUN2:** rerun the same gateway command unchanged — wait for `deploy-commands:done`

  - Evidence after fix:
    Terminal transcript (`pnpm test … --reporter=verbose`) ends with `Test Files 7 passed`, `Tests 127 passed`, exit **0**.

    **Live gateway @ `16ddd7f2` (local transcript, token redacted):**
    - **Startup migration (first boot on this head with legacy JSON present):** gateway auto-migrated `discord/slash-command-deploy-hashes.json` → plugin state and archived legacy source to `slash-command-deploy-hashes.json.migrated`.
    - **RUN1 (empty plugin-state fingerprint, commands already registered on Discord):** `deploy-commands:schedule … slashCommandDeploy=changed-only … commandCount=47`; `native-slash-command-deploy-rest:get` to `/applications/1471498116237885474/commands`; `deploy-commands:done` ~4.8s; fingerprint persisted in plugin state:
      `sqlite3 … "SELECT entry_key, value_json FROM plugin_state_entries WHERE namespace='slash-command-deploy-hashes';"` → `1471498116237885474:default` with `global:reconcile` SHA256 `0adc06bf…9297aa9`.
    - **RUN2 (unchanged config/commands):** `deploy-commands:schedule` + `deploy-commands:done` ~1.6s; **zero** `native-slash-command-deploy-rest:*` lines (REST deploy skipped).

    Key log lines (RUN1):
```
deploy-commands:schedule … slashCommandDeploy=changed-only … commandCount=47
native-slash-command-deploy-rest:get:start … path=/applications/1471498116237885474/commands
native-slash-command-deploy-rest:get:done … requestMs=715
deploy-commands:done … ~4792ms
```

    Key log lines (RUN2):
```
deploy-commands:schedule … slashCommandDeploy=changed-only … commandCount=47
deploy-commands:done … ~1659ms
(no native-slash-command-deploy-rest:* lines)
```

  - Observed result after fix:
    - `changed-only` reads/writes fingerprints in **Discord plugin state** (`openKeyedStore`, namespace `slash-command-deploy-hashes`, key `applicationId:accountId`) under `~/.openclaw-discord-proof/state/openclaw.sqlite`.
    - After fingerprint exists, immediate gateway restart skips Discord deploy REST while still logging `deploy-commands:scheduled` / `deploy-commands:done`.
    - Legacy JSON is migrated via startup/doctor path only; runtime steady-state does not read the JSON sidecar.

  - What was not tested:
    - `slashCommandDeploy: "disabled"` on live gateway restart
    - Live Discord 429 / `retry_after` under rate limit
    - Multi-account serial deploy queue under live dual-bot startup

  - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
    Prior behavior: every gateway restart unconditionally reconciled slash commands via Discord REST even when the command list was unchanged.



---

## Migration proof (commits b3282460 + 16ddd7f2)

**What was added after original live proof:**

Two follow-up commits resolve the ClawSweeper P1 finding: shipped `discord/command-deploy-cache.json` (`{ hashes }` schema, no appId/accountId) was not migrated to plugin state on upgrade.

**Fix:**
- `slash-command-deploy-state.ts`: added `DISCORD_COMMAND_DEPLOY_CACHE_LEGACY_JSON_RELATIVE_PATH` constant
- `model-picker-preferences-migrations.ts`: new migration plan reads `discord/command-deploy-cache.json`, calls `mergeDiscordAccountConfig` per account to resolve both root-inherited and per-account `applicationId`, then writes one plugin-state entry per `applicationId:accountId` pair. `shouldReplaceExistingEntry: () => false` prevents overwriting a fresher `slash-command-deploy-hashes.json` migration.

**Test run at head `7bf4b50` (macOS, Node v22.19.0, Vitest v4.1.7):**

```
pnpm test \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/model-picker-preferences-migrations.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/slash-command-deploy-state.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/internal/client.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/commands.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.startup.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts \
  -- --reporter=verbose

 Test Files  7 passed (7)
      Tests  127 passed (127)
   Start at  15:23:00
   Duration  3.17s
```

**Migration scenarios covered by new tests:**
- `command-deploy-cache.json` with root `applicationId` → single `appId:default` key
- `command-deploy-cache.json` with no `applicationId` → empty entries (graceful cold start)
- `command-deploy-cache.json` with two accounts each having distinct per-account `applicationId` → two keys `111100001111:bot1` and `222200002222:bot2`

**Doctor upgrade proof (current-head `7bf4b50`, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw 2026.6.1):**

Simulated v2026.5.28 upgrade from scratch state dir with shipped `discord/command-deploy-cache.json`:

```
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=~/.openclaw-proof-upgrade openclaw doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive
```

Doctor output:
```
[state-migrations] Auto-migrated legacy state:
- Migrated 1 Discord command deploy cache (v2026.5.28 and earlier) entry → plugin state
- Archived Discord command deploy cache (v2026.5.28 and earlier) legacy source →
  command-deploy-cache.json.migrated
```

SQLite verification after migration:
```
sqlite3 ... "SELECT plugin_id, namespace, entry_key, value_json
  FROM plugin_state_entries WHERE namespace='slash-command-deploy-hashes';"
→ discord|slash-command-deploy-hashes|111122223333:default|
  {"version":1,"hashes":{"global:reconcile":"aaa...a",
  "guild:1471499665584296039":"bbb...b"}}
```

Legacy file status:
```
$ ls discord/command-deploy-cache.json       # → No such file (migrated)
$ ls discord/command-deploy-cache.json.migrated  # → archived copy exists
```

**Combined proof:**
- Doctor --fix migrates v2026.5.28 shipped `command-deploy-cache.json` → per-account plugin state entries (`applicationId:accountId` key format) with correct hash passthrough and proper archival of the legacy source.
- Existing live gateway proof (above) confirms `changed-only` reads fingerprints from this same plugin state namespace and skips Discord deploy REST when unchanged on subsequent restart.

**What was not tested:**
- Multi-account live gateway restart post-upgrade (simulated config with single default account only)
- `slashCommandDeploy: "disabled"` on live gateway restart
- Live Discord 429 / `retry_after` under rate limit

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress silent callback fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- suppress Telegram direct-chat silent reply fallback for synthetic `callback_query` button turns
- keep answering callback queries so Telegram clears the button spinner
- preserve normal visible callback replies and non-callback silent reply behavior

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram private-chat callback button taps that intentionally return `NO_REPLY` no longer emit a visible fallback message, while non-empty callback replies still send normally and callback queries are still answered.
- **Real environment tested:** Real Telegram Bot API with throwaway bot `@O_Demo_Bot`, private Telegram chat, PR head commit `ebe2c6a3783b0aa432a5f377fe549e91850f97b4`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a Node proof harness from the PR worktree that sent an inline-keyboard message to Telegram, then tapped `Silent NO_REPLY` and `Visible reply` in the actual Telegram client.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal capture / live output from the real Telegram run:

```text
sendMessage chat=6566057320 text="PR #75857 live proof v2. Tap Silent, then Visible."
proofMessage chat=6566057320 message_id=4
callback data=proof2:silent from=6566057320 chat=6566057320 message_id=4
answerCallbackQuery id=53088534…
agentReply text="NO_REPLY"
delivery sendMessage count=0 reason=silentReplyContext group-like callback suppression
callback data=proof2:visible from=6566057320 chat=6566057320 message_id=4
answerCallbackQuery id=53088534…
agentReply text="Handled option A"
sendMessage chat=6566057320 text="Handled option A"
delivery sendMessage count=1
result silent=true visible=true
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Silent callback answered the callback query and produced `sendMessage count=0`; visible callback answered the callback query and sent exactly one `Handled option A` message.
- **What was not tested:** Full long-running OpenClaw gateway deployment was not restarted for this proof; validation used the PR branch behavior path plus real Telegram callback/query delivery.

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts -- -t callback_query`
- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(gateway): render codex inline media once
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-harness replies that emitted local `MEDIA:` image directives could fail to become trusted outgoing media, leaving inline images text-only or unavailable to WebChat/Control UI.
- Why it matters: Agents using the Codex harness should be able to return generated/local images inline without requiring channel-specific manual workarounds, and users should not see duplicate assistant turns for one image reply.
- What changed: Trust structured local media markers from non-external tool results, while preserving the existing registered-tool trust checks; replace the latest raw assistant `MEDIA:` transcript turn with the normalized media-bearing message instead of appending a second assistant image turn.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change external MCP tool trust, local media root serving checks, model prompting, ComfyUI plugin behavior, or any provider/network API.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No matching upstream issue was found. Searches checked: `inline image`, `inline media`, `Codex harness image`, `MEDIA: image`, `assistant-media`, `managed media`, `Control UI image`, and duplicate image/WebChat terms.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Codex/AppServer path can persist the model-authored assistant reply as raw text containing `MEDIA:<local path>` while the gateway reply payload carries the parsed/normalized media URL. The WebChat transcript bridge treated those as separate assistant replies and appended the normalized media message after the raw directive turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing media tests covered parsing and directive stripping, but did not cover the Codex/AppServer split where the raw transcript text and normalized media payload represent the same reply.
- Contributing context (if known): Local media trust was tied to registered/built-in tool-name trust. Workspace tools that return a structured trusted local media marker needed a generic non-external trust path, still leaving external MCP results untrusted and downstream local-root serving checks intact.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Non-external structured `trustedLocalMedia` tool results keep local media URLs, while external tool results with the same marker do not.
  - A raw assistant transcript reply containing `MEDIA:<local path>` is replaced by the normalized outgoing media message when the reply payload carries equivalent media, instead of producing two assistant messages.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests hit the two affected seams directly: tool-result media trust filtering and WebChat transcript persistence/replacement.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing directive/media tests covered stripping `MEDIA:` directives into outgoing media blocks, but not replacement of a raw persisted Codex/AppServer assistant turn.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new regression coverage is included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-harness agents can render trusted local image replies inline in WebChat/Control UI without leaving a raw `MEDIA:` line behind or showing a duplicate text-plus-image assistant reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text]
                              -> [gateway normalized media reply appended]
                              -> [duplicate assistant turns]

After:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text detected]
                              -> [latest assistant turn replaced with normalized media blocks]
                              -> [single inline image reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security notes:

- Structured local media trust is ignored for external MCP tool results.
- Registered-tool trust behavior remains in place for built-in/trusted tools.
- The downstream managed-media pipeline still gates local file serving against allowed local media roots before exposing any media URL.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development workspace; Windows gateway hot-patch was separately smoke-tested before PR preparation.
- Runtime/container: Node local workspace.

target.title: docs: Document oMLX (Apple Silicon MLX) as memorySearch embedding provider
target.body:
## Summary

Resolves the documentation gap identified in #74732. Adds focused recipes
for using oMLX (Apple Silicon MLX inference server) as a memorySearch
embedding provider via the existing OpenAI-compatible adapter.

## Changes

- `docs/concepts/memory-search.md`: New "Using oMLX" subsection under
  Quick start, plus an oMLX row in the Supported providers table.
- `docs/reference/memory-config.md`: New oMLX example under Remote
  endpoint config, with a warning against the common `provider: "ollama"`
  mistake.
- `docs/providers/openai.md`: New "Local OpenAI-compatible servers"
  subsection under Memory embeddings, surfacing oMLX as a notable
  Apple Silicon use case.

## What this addresses

Per the Codex review on #74732, this is the **narrow docs-only fix**:

> Add a focused docs recipe for oMLX that uses the existing OpenAI-compatible
> embedding path, warns users not to configure oMLX as Ollama, includes the
> local /v1 base URL and model example from the report, and tells users to
> reindex after changing embedding models or vector spaces.

All three points are covered. No core behavior change; no new provider
adapter introduced. The first-class `omlx` adapter discussion (Option B in
#74732) remains separate and is **not** part of this PR.

## Verification

The configuration shipped in this PR was verified end-to-end on:

| Component | Version |
|---|---|
| OpenClaw | 2026.4.26 |
| oMLX | v1.3.6 |
| Embedding model | jina-embeddings-v5-text-small-retrieval-mlx (1024 dims) |

## Related

Closes #74732 (docs portion).

cc @jundot — oMLX maintainer, in case the oMLX side wants to cross-link.

target.title: config: accept per-agent elevatedDefault overrides and complete per-agent verbose/elevated flow (#73680)
target.body:
## Summary

Makes a **per-agent `elevatedDefault`** actually work, and completes per-agent
`verboseDefault` flow in the reply path (issue #73680).

## The gap

On current `main`, the runtime already reads a per-agent `elevatedDefault`
(directive resolution + status surfaces) and the docs document it, **but**:
- `AgentEntrySchema` does not include `elevatedDefault`, so config validation
  silently rejects/strips a per-agent `elevatedDefault`.
- `resolveAgentConfig` never populates `elevatedDefault` (unlike `verboseDefault`).
- The reply directive resolution reads only `agentCfg.elevatedDefault` (the global
  default) and `agentCfg.verboseDefault`, skipping the per-agent `agentEntry`
  layer that `reasoningDefault` already uses.

Net effect: a documented per-agent `elevatedDefault` had no effect.

## The fix

- Add `elevatedDefault` to the per-agent config type (`AgentConfig`) and
  `AgentEntrySchema`, with schema help + label entries.
- Resolve it in `resolveAgentConfig` as `entry.elevatedDefault ?? defaults.elevatedDefault`,
  mirroring `verboseDefault`.
- In `resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels` and `resolveReplyDirectives`, read
  `agentEntry.elevatedDefault` (and `agentEntry.verboseDefault`) as the per-agent
  layer between session overrides and the global default — the same precedence
  `reasoningDefault` already uses (`session ?? agentEntry ?? global`). Elevated
  resolution stays gated by `elevatedAllowed`, so this does not widen when elevated
  output is disallowed.
- Regenerate the config doc baseline hash for the new key.

`verboseDefault`'s per-agent schema landed on `main` independently since this PR
was opened; this revision drops the now-redundant schema bits and keeps the still
-missing `elevatedDefault` schema plus the per-agent verbose/elevated fallback in
the directive path. Rebased onto current `main`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.levels.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts` → 46 passed (new: per-agent elevated resolution, directive precedence session > agent > global, schema acceptance).
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check` → OK.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` → clean. `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean.

Closes #73680

target.title: fix(exec): keep framework verb out of backtick-wrapped failure message (#97319)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Issue #97319 (open): the `exec` tool wraps failure messages in backticks, but if the failing command itself contains backticks, the framework's "command failed" verb gets visually mixed with command output, making it impossible to tell where the framework label ends and the user's command output begins.

## Why This Change Was Made

The framework verb is metadata about the failure; the user's command output is the payload. Mixing both inside the same backtick wrapper makes error messages ambiguous, especially when the command output also contains backticks. Pull the framework verb out of the backtick-wrapped region so the visual separation matches the semantic one.

## User Impact

When an `exec` command fails, the error UI now shows the framework's "command failed" label outside the user's command output. No behavior change on success path.

## Evidence

- Local: `pnpm test` on touched exec/tool-rendering test files
- Touched files: 4 files, +90/-3
- Branch: `fix/exec-failure-verb-outside-command-97319`
- Issue: fixes #97319

target.title: fix(deepinfra): bound video generation JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where the DeepInfra video generation success response was read
with an unbounded `await response.json()`, meaning a misbehaving or hostile
DeepInfra endpoint (including a self-hosted `baseUrl` override accepted by
`normalizeDeepInfraBaseUrl`) could stream an arbitrarily large JSON body into
the Node.js heap before parsing began — causing memory pressure or OOM on the
video generation path.

The error body on the same path was already bounded via
`assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, but the success JSON path was missed in the
response-limit campaign that fixed xAI, Runway, Together, Pixverse, and others.

Related: the existing `try/catch` block around `response.json()` duplicated
malformed-JSON wrapping already provided by `readProviderJsonResponse`, so this
change also removes that duplication.

## Why This Change Was Made

`readProviderJsonResponse` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`) wraps
`readResponseWithLimit` with a 16 MiB cap, cancels the ReadableStream on
overflow, and surfaces a consistent `"<label>: malformed JSON response"` error
on bad JSON — replacing the previous manual `try/catch` with a single call and
net -1 LOC on the production path. Error message format is unchanged.

## User Impact

No user-visible behavior change for well-behaved endpoints. Operators running
DeepInfra video generation against a self-hosted or compromised `baseUrl` that
returns an oversized JSON payload will now see a bounded error
(`Content too large: N bytes (limit: 16777216 bytes)`) instead of unbounded
heap growth.

## Evidence

**Diff** — `extensions/deepinfra/video-generation-provider.ts` (net -1 LOC):

```diff
-        let payload: DeepInfraVideoResponse;
-        try {
-          payload = (await response.json()) as DeepInfraVideoResponse;
-        } catch (cause) {
-          throw new Error("DeepInfra video generation failed: malformed JSON response", { cause });
-        }
+        const payload = await readProviderJsonResponse<DeepInfraVideoResponse>(
+          response,
+          "DeepInfra video generation failed",
+        );
```

**Proof script** (`node scripts/proof-deepinfra-bound.mjs`, Node v22.22.0):

```
[case 1] oversized DeepInfra video JSON, cap=1024 KiB, ReadableStream
  ok: readResponseWithLimit rejected on oversized body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: Content too large: 2097152 bytes (limit: 1048576 bytes)) — true
  ok: stream was cancelled before all 64 chunks were read (readCount=2) — true
  ok: stream cancel() was called — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded await response.json() — no cancellation
  ok: unbounded .json() consumed all chunks before failing (readCount=64) — true
  ok: stream NOT cancelled via .cancel() — drained to EOF instead — true

[case 3] normal small DeepInfra video JSON — parses unchanged
  ok: small body parsed into result — true
  ok: result content intact (req_proof_ok present) — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

**Local test suite** (`pnpm test extensions/deepinfra`, Vitest v4.1.8, Node v22.22.0):

```
Test Files  12 passed (12)
     Tests  73 passed (73)
  Duration  23.76s
```

AI-assisted.
target.author: hugenshen
